anchor.snippet: Combinatorial game theory is a branch of mathematics and theoretical computer science
A key concept in combinatorial game theory is that of the solved game
tic-tac-toe is solved in that optimal play by both participants always results in a draw
in 2007, checkers was announced to be weakly solved
It is useful to distinguish between combinatorial "mathgames"
the theory of impartial games , in which any play available to one player must be available to the other as well
subject to the normal play condition , which means that a player who cannot move loses
the Sprague–Grundy theorem showed that all impartial games are equivalent to heaps in Nim
jointly introduced the theory of a partisan game , in which the requirement that a play available to one player be available to both is relaxed
the sum of two games, which is a game where each player may choose to move either in one game or the other at any point in the game
Blue–Red Hackenbush - At the finite level, this partisan combinatorial game allows constructions of games whose values are dyadic rational numbers
Blue–Red–Green Hackenbush - Allows for additional game values that are not numbers in the traditional sense
Toads and Frogs - Allows various game values
Domineering - Various interesting games, such as hot games , appear as positions in Domineering
Nim - An impartial game

Up , written as ↑, is a position in combinatorial game theory

annotations.label: Up positive, down negative, both infinitesimal

anchor.snippet: Up is strictly positive (↑ > 0), and down is strictly negative
Both moves in this game are an advantage for the player who makes them; so the game is said to be "hot;"
An impartial game is one where, at every position of the game, the same moves are available to both players
the games defined in this way are known as nimbers
The Sprague–Grundy theorem states that every impartial game under the normal play convention is equivalent to a nimber
The "smallest" nimbers – the simplest and least under the usual ordering of the ordinals – are 0 and ∗
